description:
You are assisting an infrastructure engineer who is automating the very first “sanity-check” phase of a bare-metal server provisioning workflow.  
    The engineer would like one self-contained diagnostics log that captures:

       • The current system uptime  
       • A snapshot of RAM usage  
       • The current disk-space usage of the root (“/”) filesystem  

    Your goal is to create the directory “/home/user/provisioning/logs” (if it does not already exist) and write a single file called “initial_diagnostics.log” inside that directory.  
    The engineer’s automation will later parse this file, so its exact structure is crucial.

    File: /home/user/provisioning/logs/initial_diagnostics.log  
    Required format (MUST match exactly, including case, spacing and the newline placement):

        ### SYSTEM UPTIME
        <output of the command you choose that prints the current uptime>

        ### MEMORY USAGE
        <output of the command you choose that prints RAM statistics>

        ### DISK USAGE
        <output of the command you choose that prints the disk-space usage of “/”>

    Formatting rules:

    1. Each section header starts with three “#” symbols followed by a single space, the title in ALL CAPS, and a newline.  
    2. After each header, print the raw command output on one or more lines.  
    3. Leave exactly one blank line between sections (i.e., one additional newline after the last line of the preceding command output).  
    4. The headers must appear in the order shown above—no additional headers, no re-ordering.  
    5. The final file must end with a trailing newline.  
    6. Set the directory permissions to 755 (rwxr-xr-x) and the file permissions to 644 (rw-r--r--).

    Constraints & Hints:

    • Do NOT hard-code static values; capture live data from the running container.  
    • You do not have root access, so rely solely on commands that work unprivileged.  
    • You will probably use no more than three or four shell commands in total.  
    • Think about simple built-ins that reveal uptime, memory, and disk usage information without needing sudo.  

    When you have finished, the engineer’s CI pipeline will open the file and verify:

       a.  The file exists at the exact path given.  
       b.  All three headers are present and in the correct order.  
       c.  Each header is followed by at least one non-blank line of output.  

    No additional output or commentary files should be created—only “/home/user/provisioning/logs/initial_diagnostics.log”.
truth:
<!-- Ground-truth data the test harness relies on. **Do NOT reveal these outside this block.** -->

    1. Path that must exist: /home/user/provisioning/logs/initial_diagnostics.log

    2. The file must contain, in this *exact* order, the following header lines starting at the beginning of a line:
          "### SYSTEM UPTIME"
          "### MEMORY USAGE"
          "### DISK USAGE"

       They must be separated by exactly one blank line (two consecutive newlines) after the block of data that follows each header.

    3. Each header must be followed by *at least* one non-empty line that is *not* another header (to guarantee that real command output is present).

    4. Directory “/home/user/provisioning/logs” must have mode 0755.  
       File “initial_diagnostics.log” must have mode 0644.

    5. No other files should appear under /home/user/provisioning besides “logs”.
